sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Post-viral recovery may involve fatigue, breathlessness, deconditioning, orthostatic symptoms, and cognitive slowing. Rehabilitation should be paced and symptom-aware rather than automatically exercise-intensive.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Fatigue out of proportion to recent activity
- Reduced exercise tolerance, post-exertional worsening, or delayed crashes
- Breathlessness, dizziness, poor concentration, or sleep disturbance
- Difficulty resuming work, exercise, or daily responsibilities

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Screen for hypoxia, myocarditis red flags, chest pain, severe orthostatic symptoms, thromboembolic concern, or other medical complications
- Some patients tolerate graded loading well; others worsen with poorly paced exercise
- Recovery planning must respect post-exertional symptom worsening if present
- Standard graded exercise therapy (GET) is contraindicated in confirmed ME/CFS where post-exertional malaise (PEM) is present - traditional progressive exercise programmes can cause significant relapse. Follow NICE 2021 ME/CFS guidance: pacing and energy management are first-line; avoid GET in PEM-predominant presentations

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Establish current activity ceiling, rest pattern, and symptom flare behaviour
- Encourage pacing, rest distribution, hydration, and avoidance of all-or-nothing activity cycles
- Escalate for cardiopulmonary red flags, profound decline, or diagnostic uncertainty

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Build an individualized plan for graded recovery using symptom-guided progression rather than fixed timelines
- Address breathing pattern, sleep, nutrition, orthostatic symptoms, and work demands alongside exercise tolerance
- Coordinate medical review when symptoms are persistent, multisystem, or substantially disabling

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - The right pace is the fastest one that does not repeatedly crash the patient
